entry.title: What's New in 2602, Release Navigator for Spend Management
entry.content:
Welcome to Q1 2026! A new year brings fresh innovations, and we’re excited to introduce the 2602 updates for the Release Navigator for Spend Management. This update is all about enhancing content maintenance, helping you stay ahead of the release!

Tool Overview

The Release Navigator for Spend Management is a tool provided by SAP Enterprise Support designed to be your central hub for understanding and managing new software releases for SAP Ariba, SAP Business Network, SAP Fieldglass, & SAP Concur. It allows you to easily locate essential resources for upcoming releases and quickly find cross-solution materials, documentation, webcasts, and more all in one tool!

  

New & Updated Content:

  • 2602 What's New Viewer
  • 2602 Early Release Webcasts
  • 2602 Community Blogs:
    • SAP Supplier Management & Risk
    • SAP Procure-to-Order
    • SAP Source-to-Contract
    • SAP Category Management
  • 2602 Feature Spreadsheet

2602 Feature Highlights:

SAP Ariba Procurement:

  • Generating Contract Summaries in SAP Ariba Contracts using AI [SCONTR-1266]
  • Modifying Event Duration and Searching Sourcing Requests Using Joule [SOURCE-2534]
  • Additional AI Copilot Joule Capabilities for Finding Suppliers, Supplier Contacts, and Supplier Management Projects [SLP-2411]

SAP Business Network:

  • Mass Aprove Order Confirmation Deviations [CSC-69956]
  • Sending Additional Address Details from TM to BN4L [FC-282]
  • Enhancement to Buyer Groups User Interface [NP-69370]

Innovation Schedule

To ensure a smooth user experience, the Release Navigator introduces new user‑experience updates once per year in August, alongside refreshed release content.

The Release Navigator will update automatically over the coming weeks—no action is required on your end.

Disclaimer: These dates are informational only and may change. The Readiness@Scale team has final decisions over any UX modifications.

Connect with Us

Your feedback helps shape future enhancements to the Release Navigator. We invite you to:

  • Complete our feedback survey
  • Share ideas or requests in the comments section
  • Learn more about the Readiness@Scale team
  • Explore additional content

Thank you for your continued engagement and support.

Best Run,
Readiness@Scale

SAP Enterprise Support

entry.title: Balancing Conflicting Priorities of Procurement and Supply Chain
entry.content:
From Factory Floor to Forecast: What Matt Donegan’s Procurement Stories Reveal About Where SAP Customers Are Heading 🚀

If you’ve ever wondered why procurement debates can get so heated—especially in manufacturing—Matt Donegan’s podcast conversation is a masterclass in the real-world tension behind “simple” sourcing decisions. 🧩 Beneath the lighthearted dog-and-TikTok opener, Matt digs into an issue SAP customers and partners know well: direct materials procurement isn’t just about savings—it’s about keeping production running, protecting quality, and managing risk across a supply chain that’s often far less predictable than it looks on a dashboard. ⚙️

What makes Matt’s perspective especially valuable is that it’s grounded in plant-site reality. He’s lived the pressure of missing ingredients, uncertain shipments, and the ripple effect that one supplier decision can create across operations. And for organizations modernizing with SAP, his stories point to a clear message: the future belongs to teams that connect sourcing strategy with shop-floor execution—using data, collaboration, and technology that people actually adopt. 📈

Direct vs. Indirect: Why the “Lifeblood” Spend Gets Different Rules 🧠

One of the most important framing points in the discussion is the distinction between direct materials, indirect materials, and services—because the business consequences are radically different. 🏭

  • Direct materials are inputs that become part of the finished product. They’re planned against forecasts, tied to production schedules, and directly impact what customers receive on a daily basis. ✅
  • Indirect materials support the business (think facility supplies, office goods, and warehouse items), but don’t appear on the bill of materials. 🧻
  • Services keep operations running—from maintenance to repairs to cleaning and MRO-related work. 🛠️

Why does this matter to SAP customers and partners? Because direct procurement runs on a different clock and carries different risk. A late shipment of a key ingredient can halt a production line—turning a “good deal” into an expensive disruption. ⏱️

Plant Managers Aren’t Graded on Savings—They’re Graded on Output 🎯

Matt’s biggest insight is also one of the most practical: plant leaders are running a business inside the business. 📦 They manage inputs, transform them, and ship outputs—and their priority is being a reliable supplier to their customers.

From that lens, “success” looks like:

  • First-pass quality (materials meet spec without rework) ✅
  • On-time, in-full delivery (OTIF) (materials arrive when and how much is needed) 📅
  • Available-to-promise reliability (customers can trust commitments) 🤝

This is where procurement gets tricky: centralized sourcing teams may be measured primarily on year-over-year savings, while operations teams are measured on uptime and performance. 📊 The result is a familiar tug-of-war: cost optimization versus production certainty.

For SAP ecosystems, this is a reminder that transformation isn’t just system change—it’s KPI alignment and governance design. 🧭

The Real Pain Point: Global Supply Chains Still Have “Visibility Gaps” 🌍

One of Matt’s most vivid stories is about overseas procurement: sending a PO, getting a quick confirmation, and then enduring weeks of uncertainty. 🚢

Even when suppliers are reliable, international logistics introduces unpredictable variables:

  • port delays
  • customs holds
  • limited tracking precision
  • last-mile scheduling issues
  • constraints on receiving capacity at the plant 🧱

Matt highlights a dimension many teams underestimate: the human and physical reality of receiving. Materials don’t just “arrive.” Someone must unload, inspect, store safely (sometimes in specialized containers), and route it into production. 👷

For SAP customers, this underscores why visibility isn’t just a “nice-to-have.” It is operational resilience. And for partners, it reinforces the value of designs that connect procurement, logistics, inventory, and quality processes into one continuous picture. 🧩

Consignment: The Underused Strategy That Solves Two Problems at Once 💡

One of the most actionable tactics discussed is consignment inventory, which Matt calls a best friend—and for good reason. 🤝

In a consignment model:

  • the supplier ships material to the buyer’s site
  • the buyer does not pay until the material is pulled for use
  • inventory can sit nearby as a buffer without burdening working capital 💰

This creates two strategic advantages:

  1. Operational safety: the plant has backup stock when overseas shipments slip. 🛡️
  2. Working capital relief: millions in inventory can be available without sitting on the buyer’s balance sheet. 📉

The catch? Consignment depends on strong supplier trust—typically built through years of partnership and reliable consumption patterns. 🧱 This is an important point for procurement leaders: resilience often comes from relationship maturity, not just contract terms.

The Collaboration Playbook: Data, Shared Strategy, and Stakeholder Buy-In 📊

When asked how organizations can reduce friction between central sourcing and operations, Matt returns to a simple idea: be data-driven—and bring stakeholders along early. 🧾

He describes an internal “negotiation” process between sourcing and plant teams:

  • which suppliers are non-negotiable due to quality risk
  • where contracts can be rebalanced without harming performance
  • how allocation (local vs. overseas) can hedge risk over time ⚖️

The critical enablers here are:

  • shared metrics (not just siloed KPIs) 📐
  • supplier performance data (quality, OTIF, rework rates) 🔍
  • tools people actually use (adoption matters as much as capability) ✅

For SAP customers, this is a blueprint: procurement transformation succeeds when the system supports cross-functional decision-making—not when it simply digitizes old silos. 🏗️

Future-Facing Take: AI Will Elevate the Human Side of Procurement 🤖➡️🧑‍🤝‍🧑

Matt’s forward-looking statement is refreshingly balanced: yes, automation will expand—especially across tactical work and data-heavy tasks. 🤖 But his “hot take” is that what will differentiate procurement professionals is not purely technical mastery.

Instead, the standouts will be those who excel at:

  • Stakeholder influence internally 🗣️
  • Strategic supplier relationships externally 🤝
  • Joint innovation with partners 💡
  • Risk mitigation through trust, audits, and shared plans 🛡️

In other words: as AI absorbs more of the “math,” procurement becomes even more human. And that’s a powerful lens for SAP customers investing in intelligent procurement: the goal isn’t replacing people—it’s freeing them to do higher-value work. 🚀

Conclusion: The Next Procurement Advantage Is Integration—Of Data and People ✅

Matt Donegan’s stories remind us that procurement isn’t a back-office function in manufacturing—it’s a lever that can protect uptime, improve quality, and reduce volatility across the supply chain. 🏭

For SAP customers and partners, the implications are clear:

  • Align KPIs across sourcing and operations to reduce internal conflict 🎯
  • Use data to shift debates from opinion to evidence 📊
  • Build supplier partnerships that unlock resilience tools like consignment 🤝
  • Invest in technology that supports collaboration—not just compliance 🧩
  • Prepare for AI-enabled procurement where relationship skills matter more than ever 🤖✨

If there’s one takeaway worth reflecting on, it’s this: the best procurement strategies don’t optimize a number—they optimize the business. 📈

https://podcasts.apple.com/us/podcast/episode-027-aligning-procurement-and-supply-chain/id1764078646?i=1000749416873

https://open.spotify.com/show/5twXP1NehSqgCAznTnzQ5H

entry.title: Troubleshooting Ariba-Joule Integration errors
entry.content:
Introduction:🚀 Cracking the SAP Ariba–Joule Integration common errors:

Over the past few months, I’ve seen a common pattern across multiple customer implementations: brilliant landscapes struggling with Navigation Flow glitches, incomplete Transactional prompts, or inconsistent User Sync—all caused by a handful of tiny but critical configuration gaps across Ariba, IAS, IPS, and Build Work Zone.

To help accelerate troubleshooting and avoid post‑go‑live surprises, I’ve put together a practical breakdown of the most recurring issues and the exact fixes that have consistently stabilized the integration.

If you're enabling Joule for Ariba or supporting customers on their journey, this article will save you hours of guesswork. 🔧💡

Common issues with Navigation flow and resolution: 

Scenario 1:Navigational prompts intermittently return data

Sample prompts: Go my approvals, my task, create engagement request, created sourcing project, open supplier profile etc   

Cause: This could happen, if user has been assigned more than 150 groups in Ariba and those groups are being replicated to IAS and then to Build work zone.  To verify if the issue is due to roles replicated to BWZ,  login to BWZ and user will get error “HTTP ERROR 431”. 

Resolution: Replicate only those standard groups to BWZ which are required for Ariba-Joule integration. You can achieve this by adding a filter parameter ariba.applications.group.filter in the IPS source system (the source system configured to fetch users and groups from Ariba https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/joule-integration-with-sap-ariba-setup-guide/ba-p/14240003 ). Using this filter ensures that only the required groups—and users assigned to those groups—are synchronized from Ariba to IAS and subsequently to BWZ.

Sample value: (displayName eq "Sourcing Agent" or displayName eq "Sourcing Approver")

Important Notes

  • Navigational flow works only with standard groups.
  • If customers assign only custom roles to users in Ariba, they must still replicate the required standard groups from Ariba → IAS → BWZ.
  • Custom groups are not supported, SAP is considering for future release 
  • This filtering process does not impact customer‑specific role assignments:
    • Customers may continue using their custom groups assignment in Ariba as usual
    • Since custom groups map back to standard roles, any Joule‑relevant standard roles tied to custom roles will still function properly through this filtered sync.
  • Recommendation:
    • Maintain a list of standard groups required for Joule navigation.
    • Additionally replicate customer‑specific custom groups only to ensure that all Ariba users sync correctly to CIS

Steps to maintain the parameter:

  • Login to CIS and navigate to identity provision
  • Source system à look for the source system which is created to fetch the user/groups from Ariba
  • Edit and maintain the parameter and run the IPS job as described previously
List of standard roles which are required for Ariba joule Navigational scenario:

Supplier Registration Manager

Supplier Manager

Supplier Qualification Manager

Supplier Risk ESG User

Purchasing Administrator

Supplier Risk Engagement Requestor

Financial Processing Analyst

Supplier Risk Evaluation Expert

AuditBuyer

Supplier Risk Provider Registration Expert

Purchasing Agent

Joule Supplier Profile User

Invoice Manager

Sourcing Project Administrator

Purchasing Manager

Sourcing Agent

Report Manager

Limited Event Administrator

Receiving Manager

Junior Sourcing Agent

Manual Order Processor

SM Questionnaire Manager

Tax Manager

Supplier Risk Engagement Governance Analyst

Purchasing User

Supplier Qualification Request Manager

Executive Approver

Supplier Risk User

Sourcing Approver

Supplier Risk Report Expert

Category Buyer

Supplier Risk Engagement Expert

Supplier Risk Engagement Analyst

Sourcing Manager

Operational Junior Sourcing Agent

Supplier Risk Manager

Supplier Risk Configuration Expert

Supplier Request Manager

SM Process Manager

Event Administrator

Scenario 2:  Error “The application cannot be opened”

Resolution: Create a destination under the sub-account which has been created for joule. Make sure destination name is “NavigationService” follow the below article for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/joule/integrating-joule-with-sap/configure-destination-to-use-navigation-service?locale=en-US&q=+NavigationService

Steps:

  • Login to Joule Sub-account
  • Click connectivity à Destination
  • Click create

Scenario 3: Error: You need different permissions to perform this action or I am having trouble processing your request.

Cause : This happen when user doesn’t have the required access in Ariba or if they have the required access, joule related standard roles are not assigned to user in BWZ after replication.  

To verify the roles in BWZ, login to BWZ

  • Check User Role Assignments: In the settings à Identity Provisioning
  • Within "Explore Role Assignments",enter the user ID experiencing the issue
  • Review the list of roles and permissions assigned to the user

Resolution: As described in scenario 1 navigation flow, maintain the joule roles in source system parameter run the job. This will synchronize the user and groups between ariba , IAS and BWZ. Make sure roles are assigned to users in ariba as well. Login to BWZ and verify the roles once again:

Common issues with Transactional flow and resolution:

Scenario 1:When user enters any transactional prompt in joule, user gets results as an informational data.

Sample prompts: List of recent purchases

Cause: This happens when ibn_target not found at the backend. In simple words, the formation synchronization did not happen between the include system despite the fact status of the formation is ready.  

Resolution: Login to global account, exclude and include Ariba buyer and sourcing system within the formations.

 

Scenario 2: When user enters a transactional prompt, there is no response

Cause: This happens when there is no dependencies added to the das-ias applications in CIS

Resolution:

  • Login to IAS
  • Navigate to applications and search for “das-ias” and look for the entry which is there for the sub-account which is created for joule
  • Navigate to dependency section, make sure to maintain APIs for Procurement and Sourcing applications

Delayed User synchronization between Ariba and Cloud identify service: IPS Jobs are getting stuck in running status for few hours  

Steps to run the Job:

  • Login to CIS and navigate to identity provision

Source system à look for the source system which is created to fetch the user/groups from Ariba

 Click on job logs in IPS to check the status:

 Resolution:  By default the value configured for parameter (ariba.applications.group.members.page.size,ariba.applications.user.groups.page.size )  is 50, update it according to your need. If this doesn’t work, log a support ticket with SAP to increase the API rate limit.

Joule Icon Is Missing in Ariba despite completing all the configurations (running boosters, system added successfully in formations, SSO is enabled)

Resolution: Log a ticket with SAP to enable the feature PLUI 2928  at parent and child level

If you are facing any other issues, I would suggest to log a case with support team. To save time, make sure to share the logs and X-Correlation ID with support team. 

How to download the Joule Logs:

  • Login to Ariba application
  • Click on joule icon, reproduce the issue
  • Click on three bars --> settings  --> abouts --> download trances --> include message history --> download 

How to Get X Correlation ID?

  • Login to Ariba system
  • Enable logs:
    • Right-click on browser, select "Inspect"
    • once the Developer Tools are open, locate and click on the "Network" tab
  • Open Joule (keep the Developer Tools open)
  • Reproduce the issue you are experiencing
  • When the issue is reproduced:
    • in the Network tab, identify the most recent API call to Joule. This API call will be named with a UUID (for example: c1234b04-c313-42cd-9be3-a5efe9b0f2d1).
    • click on the API request and go to the "Headers" section;
    • scroll down to find and collect the following fields:
      • X-correlationid
      • Timestamp,
      • JouleDataCenter (you can find this in the "API Request URL")

Ariba-Joule support queues: you may choose the queue based on the nature of the error. 

✨Conclusion
The most common Ariba–Joule issues often stem from small but critical configuration gaps across Ariba, IAS, IPS, and Build Work Zone—and fixing these resolves the majority of navigation, transactional, and sync failures.
With the right configurations in place, Joule delivers the seamless, intelligent experience it’s designed for—helping customers unlock real value without post‑go‑live roadblocks. 🚀

entry.title: SAP Ariba Supplier Management & Risk 2605 Release Highlights
entry.content:
Hello SAP Ariba Supplier Management & Risk (SM&R) Community!

If you're interested in learning about some of the new features coming with our May release, you're in the right place. 

Let's take a look at what's new within SM&R this quarter...

 

SAP Ariba Supplier Lifecycle and Performance

Additional Authorizations for AI Copilot Joule Scenarios in Supplier Management (SLP-2412)

This feature refines the requirements for user group membership to see prompts for specific Joule supplier management scenarios. This refinement ensures that prompts related to specific aspects of supplier management functionality are only visible  to users with relevant permissions, in customer landscapes that include the applicable solutions.

 

 

 

Ability to Restrict the Number of Commodity, Region, and Department Answers in External Questionnaires (SLP-2382)

This feature enables you to configure the maximum number of answers process initiators can add to Commodity, Region and Department questions in the external modular questionnaire.

Process initiators can only add the maximum number of answers you specify when creating a process, ensuring that processes meet your business requirements. It is applicable for external modular questionnaire. If the data is pre-populated from the registration stage or initial values specified from the template, the selected items need to be within the defined levels in the template configuration. If the pre-populated answers exceed the specified range, you must remove the values that are outside of the defined levels in the template configuration. 

 

 

SAP Ariba Supplier Risk

Additional Joule Capabilities in SAP Ariba Supplier Risk (SRISK-1860)

Your AI copilot Joule integrates with the Risk Exposure API - making it that much faster to find information via natural language prompting.

Take a look at these examples: 

 

Enhanced Virus scanning capability in Supplier Risk (SRISK-1329)

This feature automatically scans all files uploaded during different stages of an engagement request for viruses and malware, providing enhanced security protection throughout the supplier onboarding process. When documents are uploaded during any stage of creating an engagement request, each file is scanned in real-time before the next upload can begin. This sequential upload process ensures thorough security screening of documents.

Previously, you could upload multiple files consecutively without interruption. The system now requires a scan to complete before you can upload the next file. Please allow a brief moment between file uploads.

 

 

 

Enhancing Risk Exposure API by Adding Standard and Custom Category in Response (SRISK-1856)

 This feature enables the Risk Exposure API to retrieve responses for both standard and custom risk categories for suppliers you monitor in SAP Ariba Supplier Risk. The responses depend on the parameter setting for Enable custom risk categories (Application.SR.Risk.EnableCustomRiskCategories) on your site.

The current version (v3) of Risk Exposure API returns responses regardless of the parameter setting. 

When the Enable custom risk categories parameter is enabled, the API returns data for your custom risk categories.

When the parameter is disabled, the API automatically returns data for the default risk categories: Environmental & Social, Financial, Operational, and Regulatory & Legal.

(The previous version (v2) of the Risk Exposure API returned responses only when the Enable custom risk categories parameter was enabled.)

 

Once these features are officially released (May 15th) you can locate additional implementation and technical details, by simply searching for the feature ID here: What's New (via SAP Help Portal)

 

 

​Stay tuned for our additional upcoming blog posts and follow me to stay informed on upcoming feature releases across SAP Ariba.

​​Thank you for being a part of the SAP Ariba community and we look forward to continuing our journey of innovation together!

 

 

​​

 

Resources:

What’s New - where you can find implementation and Technical Details for any of these features (once released on May 15)

SAP Ariba Connect - Release Readiness (Customers except CIM)

Readiness Community page (For CIM customers)

SAP Community for Intelligent Spend Management page

SAP Help Portal pages - Procurement and Network

entry.title: Unified Supplier Management STAT
entry.content:
Imagine you are a heart surgeon about to begin a complex procedure. The operating room is prepped and the patient is ready.

At that exact moment, you have to send an email to a primary care physician for records, call a cardiologist for a diagnosis, and frantically search online for the latest AI-driven surgical planning tools.

Sounds ridiculous, right? But for many procurement professionals, this "emergency room" scramble for supplier information is a daily reality.

For many procurement organizations today, supplier data is scattered across disconnected ERPs, niche apps, spreadsheets, and literal file cabinets. Performance data—if it exists—is often unstructured, making "apples-to-apples" comparisons impossible. Risk monitoring usually requires logging into unfamiliar third-party systems, and collaboration is a disjointed, repetitive mess for both you and your suppliers.

Why does this matter? Because in the age of AI and autonomous procurement, your results are only as good as your data. AI agents can’t improve your throughput or efficiency if they are working with a fragmented "medical record."

To quantify the impact of this fragmented systems and data approach, SAP partnered with Ardent Partners - a next-generation analyst firm that leverages its data-driven research to help business executives make smarter decisions and achieve better results - to research dozens of procurement organizations. The findings were clear. Companies that prioritize a unified approach to supplier management see incremental value across a number of KPIs including:

  • 22% more compliant spend
  • 61% more enabled suppliers
  • 73% higher savings targets

Download the paper here and register to join us 28 May 2026 for the Ardent Partners webinar, Supplier Management: The Integrated Advantage where we'll dive into the research findings to reveal why integrated supplier management consistently outperforms across cost, risk, and value metrics. You will also hear a firsthand perspective from Sonae Arauco on their journey toward unified supplier management and how it aligns with broader enterprise goals.

In the operating room, success depends on having the right information at the right time. Your procurement strategy deserves that same level of precision and readiness.

entry.title: Your S/4HANA & Ariba implementation is live, but your GRC ruleset do not have Ariba rules configured
entry.content:
Most SAP GRC implementations have a blind spot.

It is called Ariba.

And if your SoD ruleset doesn't account for it, you may be signing off clean access reviews on a system that's wide open to procurement fraud.

Across numerous SAP GRC implementations and Pre Go-Live and Post Go-Live reviews across the region, I typically get one of three responses when I ask whether the SoD ruleset covers cross-application risks between S/4HANA and Ariba:

  • "SAP GRC doesn't support integration with Ariba."
  • "We don't have IAG (Identity Access Governance)."
  • "We are planning to do it as part of Phase 2."

Let me translate those for you:

  • means "we haven't figured it out yet"
  • means "we know it's a gap but haven't prioritised it yet"
  • means "we are hoping the auditors don't notice before Phase 2"

That’s not a configuration gap. That’s your next audit finding !!!

The Integration Gap

1. GRC has no native Ariba connector.

Unlike the standard S/4HANA connector that pulls user assignments, role assignments, and authorisation objects, Ariba is opaque to GRC. There's no out-of-the-box integration. 

2. Ariba doesn't expose authorisations the way S/4HANA does.

There is no PFCG equivalent where you can drill into what a user can actually do in Ariba. No authorisation objects. No field-level permissions. Just system groups with pre-defined permissions.

3. In practice, this means a user could be registering fictitious suppliers in Ariba and processing invoices in S/4HANA and your GRC system would see nothing wrong.

So What Can You Do?

Here's the practical workaround I have implemented across multiple clients. The workaround is elegant in its simplicity where we bring Ariba into GRC's language by representing Ariba groups as custom Tcodes and template roles in S/4HANA.

To gain a better understanding of the SAP Ariba authorisation design, please review the list of access groups available in Ariba using the link below.

https://help.sap.com/docs/strategic-sourcing/strategic-sourcing-and-supplier-management-group-descriptions/strategic-sourcing-and-supplier-management-group-descriptions

SAP Ariba uses a group-based authorisation model where access is determined entirely by group membership.

Standard groups delivered in Ariba have fixed, pre-defined permissions that cannot be modified. While customers can create custom groups, these are essentially collections of standard groups (through group nesting) and do not allow individual permissions or actions to be customised, unlike PFCG roles.

Unlike PFCG roles, you cannot hand-pick permissions across Ariba groups. The permission boundary is always the standard groups and custom groups are simply collections of those standard groups.

For cross-application SoD analysis, the assessment should be performed between the user's assigned Ariba groups and their S/4HANA roles/access. As Ariba access is governed by group membership, the effective permissions granted by the Ariba groups should be evaluated together with the user's S/4HANA access to identify potential SoD conflicts.

Example

This is a standard SAP Ariba system group. If I want to create a custom group called "Supplier Onboarding Team Member (SUPP_ONB_TEAM_MEM)", I would create the custom group and assign the relevant system group as its parent group. The custom group would then inherit the permissions from that system group.

The Solution

Step 1: Create Custom Tcodes for Ariba System Groups. A sample is shown in the below screenshot. The Tcode descriptions can be written in simple, business-friendly language so that end users can easily understand the purpose of each group without requiring technical knowledge.

Step 2: Create template or dummy roles for ARIBA groups in S4HANA. Let us assume that you have an ARIBA group with name "Supplier Onboarding Team Member", you can create a PFCG role as per your own naming convention.

ARIBA GroupS/4HANA role nameDescription
Supplier Onboarding Team MemberAR:S_SUPPLIER_ONBOARDING_TEAMSee screenshot below

Maintain a comprehensive description of the role, including all actions that can be performed by users assigned to it in Ariba. This enables more effective access reviews, role design validation, and SoD risk assessments.

With the above approach, SAP Ariba groups are represented as template (dummy) roles in SAP S/4HANA, while Ariba system groups are represented as custom Tcodes in SAP S/4HANA.

Step 3:  This is the critical step. Without it, Steps 1 and 2 are just housekeeping. Mapping the Ariba Tcodes into your GRC ruleset is what transforms a workaround into a functioning control.  This will enable access risk analysis to identify potential Segregation of Duties (SoD) conflicts and critical access risks arising from Ariba groups. The custom Ariba Tcodes should be mapped to existing business functions and risk definitions within the Ruleset. New functions and risks may also be created to address Ariba-specific activities.

This approach ensures that Ariba access assignments, whether provisioned directly or through composite roles, are included in access risk analysis and remain subject to the client's governance, risk, and compliance controls.

Here is a real SoD risk that spans both systems and would be completely missed without this approach. Maintain a fictitious vendor and enter a Vendor invoice for automatic payment

  • Vendor Master Maintenance (ZPR01) + Process Vendor Invoices (ZAP02)

I have included the ARIBA Tcodes related to the supplier maintenance in this Vendor Master Maintenance function.

When a user who has access to process vendor invoices is being granted with the Supplier Onboarding Team Member (Ref S4HANA Role: AR:S_SUPPLIER_ONBOARDING_TEAM) group in ARIBA, the corresponding SOD risks will be highlighted as shown in the below screenshot.

The SoD risk didn't exist in S/4HANA alone. It didn't exist in Ariba alone. It existed in the combination of access across both systems and was  invisible to  GRC ruleset, invisible to most access reviews, and invisible to auditors who don't know to look for it.

That's the gap this approach closes.

Your GRC ruleset should reflect the reality of how your users actually work across applications and not within silos. If Ariba is part of your procurement landscape and those functions are not in your ruleset, your SoD analysis is not complete.

Don't let your blind spot become your audit finding.

Extending the GRC Ruleset — What Else Can You Define?

The supplier onboarding example above is just one of many cross-system SoD conflicts that become visible once Ariba is brought into your GRC ruleset.

Using the same custom Tcodes approach, you can define risks across the full procurement lifecycle. Here are some examples:

ARIBA AccessS/4HANA AccessPotential Risk Scenario
Register / onboard suppliersExecute payment runFictitious vendor fraud
Electronically sign contractsProcess vendor invoicesContract approval + Invoice processing by same person
Create sourcing projects & requisitionsPost goods receiptsFull P2P cycle without segregation
Mass update contractsMaintain purchase ordersBulk reprice contracts then self-raise POs

The pattern is consistent i.e., the fraud doesn't live in one system. It lives in the combination.

Once your Ariba groups are represented as custom Tcodes in GRC, any of these risks can be configured, monitored and reported just like any native S/4HANA SoD conflict.

Thank you for reading. I hope this gives you a practical starting point for bridging the Ariba–S/4HANA SoD coverage gap in your GRC implementation.

If you have questions, have implemented something similar, or want to share your own approach, please drop a comment below.

You can also reach me directly at Maddysmail26@gmail.com whether it's a quick question, a deeper implementation challenge, or just a conversation about SAP.

Let's continue learning, sharing and building on each other's experience 🙂

entry.title: SAP Ariba and SAP Fieldglass Earn 2026 TrustRadius Top Rated and Buyer's Choice Awards
entry.content:
We're proud to share that SAP Ariba and SAP Fieldglass have each earned two prestigious 2026 TrustRadius awards, recognition that means a great deal because it comes entirely from the people who matter most: our customers.

 

 SAP Ariba has been named a 2026 TrustRadius Top Rated and 2026 Buyer's Choice winner in the Procurement category, while SAP Fieldglass has received the same honors in the Vendor Management category.

 

 

What These Awards Mean

TrustRadius awards are unlike many industry accolades. The Top Rated award is not a participation award, but rather it is hard-earned and based entirely on active customer sentiment, with no analyst input and no preference for TrustRadius customers. The Buyer's Choice award goes even further, signifying that a product goes above and beyond in meeting expectations across capabilities, value, and customer relationships.

In short: these awards aren't given — they're earned, review by review, from verified users sharing their real experiences.

Recognized by the People Who Use These Products Every Day

For SAP Ariba, customers consistently highlight its ability to streamline end-to-end procurement, from sourcing and contracting to supplier management and compliance. For SAP Fieldglass, users point to its strength in managing the full contingent workforce lifecycle, from onboarding external workers to time tracking, statements of work, and payment.

Earning recognition in both the Top Rated and Buyer's Choice categories across Procurement and Vendor Management reflects SAP's ongoing commitment to delivering Spend Management solutions that drive real business value, not just on paper, but in practice, for the organizations relying on these tools every day.

Thank You to Our Customers

These awards belong to the customers who took the time to share their honest feedback on TrustRadius. Your voices shape how the market understands these products, and they inspire us to keep improving.

If you'd like to explore what customers are saying about SAP Ariba or SAP Fieldglass, visit their profiles on TrustRadius and read the verified reviews firsthand.
